comprehensive_summary:
Franz Kafka is a German-speaking Czech author known for his novels The Trial, The Castle, and Amerika, and his short fiction including The Metamorphosis, 'In the Penal Colony,' 'A Hunger Artist,' 'A Report to an Academy,' 'Josephine the Singer,' 'The Judgment,' 'Investigations of a Dog,' 'The Burrow,' and many others. His friend Max Brod preserved and posthumously published most of his work, against Kafka's explicit wishes.

The Metamorphosis is his most famous work and the most common giveaway. In it, Gregor Samsa wakes up transformed into a monstrous vermin (or insect). His sister Grete initially feeds him milk (which he discovers he no longer likes) and moves his furniture so he can climb walls. His father throws an apple that becomes lodged in his back, eventually contributing to his death. Three bearded lodgers leave the house after encountering Gregor. Grete eventually suggests getting rid of him, after which Gregor plans his death. The final sentence contains a word meaning either 'traffic' or 'sexual intercourse' ('an unending stream of traffic was just going over the bridge'). Gabriel Garcia Marquez said Kafka's opening line showed him that people were 'allowed to write things like that.'

The Trial follows the bank worker Josef K., who is arrested by two unnamed government agents for an unknown crime. He receives advice from the sickly lawyer Herr Huld and the cynical painter Titorelli. A priest tells him the parable 'Before the Law,' in which a man with a 'long, thin, Tatar beard' lets himself take bribes from a man he eventually tells 'No one but you could gain admittance.' Josef K. discovers two policemen being flogged in the junkroom of a bank. At the end, he is taken to a quarry and killed, exclaiming 'Like a dog!' The story 'A Dream' features Josef K. seeing a gravedigger etch his name on a tombstone. He has an affair with Miss Burstner and is killed just before his 31st birthday.

The Castle follows the land surveyor K., who fails to gain access to the mysterious title structure. The elusive bureaucrat Klamm works there, and a messenger named Barnabas relays letters from him. K. meets two nearly identical servants, both called 'Arthur.' A barmaid breaks off her engagement to K. A village fire brigade puts on a celebration.

Amerika (also called The Man Who Disappeared) follows Karl Rossmann's arrival in the title country. Its first chapter was published separately as 'The Stoker,' in which Karl develops a connection with the title worker aboard a steamship. Karl sees the Statue of Liberty wielding a sword. The last chapter describes Karl traveling to the Nature-Theater of Oklahoma, which promises a job to anyone who applies. Gyorgy Lukacs contrasted Kafka with Thomas Mann, and critics noted Kafka's debt to Dickens in this novel.

'In the Penal Colony' is one of Kafka's most frequently clued short stories. An officer shows the Traveler a torture device consisting of a Bed, Harrow, and Inscriber that carves words into prisoners' bodies. The device inscribes 'Be Just' on the Officer's back before malfunctioning and killing him. A servant falls asleep on duty and is whipped by his captain. The old Commandant's grave is hidden under a table in a teahouse, with an inscription prophesying he will rise again. The new Commandant withholds funding for the machine. At the end, a man threatens characters with a heavy rope to keep them from jumping into his boat. Deleuze and Guattari viewed the torture device as a prototypical 'desiring machine.' Walter Benjamin discusses this story in Illuminations.

'A Hunger Artist' depicts a performer who starves himself professionally. A man distributes photographs showing the hunger artist at the fortieth day of fasting. He admits on his deathbed he never found food he liked eating. At the end, a panther replaces him in his cage. David Foster Wallace's essay 'Some Remarks on Kafka's Funniness' describes this as a 'radical literalization' of the trope 'starved for attention.'

'A Report to an Academy' is narrated by the ape Red Peter, who describes being captured by the firm of Hagenbeck on the Gold Coast, learning to drink schnapps (burned by a sailor with a pipe when unsuccessful), and choosing the Music Hall over freedom. He recalls living in a three-sided cage on a steamer.

'Josephine the Singer, or the Mouse Folk' features a mouse who shortens her whistling-like performances and whose 'piping' is questioned by the narrator. She gets out of work by claiming artistic inclinations require rest. During her performances, Mouse Folk are able to dream. She eventually disappears.

'Investigations of a Dog' features an unnamed canine attempting to understand where food comes from. The narrator meets seven musical dogs walking on their hind legs and asks 'Whence does the earth procure this food?'

'The Judgment' features Georg Bendemann, whose father lambasts his upcoming marriage to Frieda Brandenfeld and accuses him of wanting him dead, compelling Georg to jump off a bridge and drown.

'The Great Wall of China' explains the piecemeal construction of the wall, built to protect from northern nomads. The embedded parable 'An Imperial Message' concerns a herald who cannot leave an enormous palace after being entrusted with an emperor's dying message.

'The Burrow' features a creature obsessed with security and silence, describing the 'moss covering' separating it from the outside world. Kafka also wrote 'The Vulture' (a man being eaten by a bird), 'The Cares of a Family Man' (about the mysterious Odradek, a 'flat star-shaped spool for thread'), 'The Hunter Gracchus' (a dead hunter wandering on his boat, telling a burgomaster), 'A Country Doctor' (receiving mysterious horses from a man, imagining the rape of his maid Rosa), 'Blumfeld, an Elderly Bachelor' (menacing bouncing balls), and 'The New Advocate' (a horse becomes a lawyer).

Biographically, Kafka wrote a vindictive 100-page letter to his father (Letter to His Father) explaining why he broke off his engagement to Felice Bauer. He recounted being locked out on a balcony as punishment for crying for water. His diaries were begun at Max Brod's suggestion and contain drafts of his first collection, Contemplation. The Zurau Aphorisms include 'A cage went in search of a bird.' His own drawings were compiled in a 2022 book by Andreas Kilcher. Borges' essay 'Kafka and his Precursors' argues that writers create their own precursors. W.G. Sebald calls him 'Dr. K' in Vertigo. Herta Muller was compared to Kafka for similar use of German as a minority language.

works.name: The Metamorphosis
works.description: The Metamorphosis is Kafka's most famous work and the standard giveaway. Gregor Samsa wakes up transformed into a monstrous vermin. His sister Grete feeds him, his father throws an apple into his back, and he eventually dies after Grete suggests removing him. Three bearded lodgers leave after encountering him.

clues.clue: Gregor Samsa wakes up transformed into a monstrous vermin/insect/bug
quotes: Gregor Samsa wakes up transformed into a monstrous vermin in which author's story?
this author who imagined Gregor's transformation into a giant bug

quotes: a character who has an apple thrown by his father lodged in his back

quotes: only receives food from his sister, Grete

quotes: three bearded lodgers leave a house after encountering a character with an apple lodged in his back

quotes: a word meaning either 'traffic' or 'sexual intercourse' in its final sentence, 'an unending stream of traffic was just going over the bridge'

clues.clue: Gregor imagines kissing his sister's neck as she plays violin for lodgers
quotes: its protagonist imagining kissing his sister's neck, as she plays the violin for their lodgers

quotes: Gabriel Garcia Marquez began writing fiction after an opening line by this author showed him that people were 'allowed to write things like that.'

works.name: In the Penal Colony
works.description: 'In the Penal Colony' features a torture device consisting of a Bed, Harrow, and Inscriber that carves words into prisoners' bodies. The Officer has it inscribe 'Be Just' on his own back before it malfunctions and kills him. The old Commandant's grave is under a table in a teahouse. Deleuze and Guattari viewed it as a prototypical 'desiring machine.'

works.name: A Report to an Academy
works.description: 'A Report to an Academy' is narrated by the ape Red Peter, who describes his capture by the firm of Hagenbeck on the Gold Coast, learning to drink schnapps, and choosing the Music Hall over freedom. He gives a report on how he achieved human-level intelligence.

works.name: Josephine the Singer
works.description: 'Josephine the Singer, or the Mouse Folk' features a mouse who shortens her whistling-like performances. She gets out of work by claiming artistic needs. Her 'piping' is questioned by the narrator. During performances, Mouse Folk dream.

works.name: The Judgment
works.description: 'The Judgment' features Georg Bendemann, whose father accuses him of wanting him dead and lambasts his upcoming marriage to Frieda Brandenfeld. A merchant's fiancee criticizes him for having a friend in St. Petersburg. Georg then jumps off a bridge.

clues.clue: Father lambasts Georg Bendemann's upcoming marriage to Frieda Brandenfeld and accuses him of wanting him dead; Georg jumps off a bridge

clues.clue: A merchant's fiancee criticizes him for having a friend with a failing business in St. Petersburg
quotes: a merchant's fiancee criticizes him for having a friend with a failing business in St. Petersburg who won't attend their wedding

works.name: Other Short Stories
works.description: Kafka wrote many other notable short stories. 'Investigations of a Dog' features a canine attempting to understand where food comes from and meeting seven musical dogs. 'The Burrow' features a creature obsessed with security. 'The Great Wall of China' explains piecemeal construction and contains the parable 'An Imperial Message.' 'The Hunter Gracchus' features a dead hunter wandering on his boat. 'A Country Doctor' describes receiving mysterious horses and the rape of maid Rosa. 'The Cares of a Family Man' concerns the mysterious Odradek. 'The Vulture' features a man being eaten alive by a bird. 'A Little Fable' has a mouse lamenting that 'the whole world is growing smaller every day.'

clues.clue: 'Investigations of a Dog': a canine tries to understand where food comes from; meets seven musical dogs; asks 'Whence does the earth procure this food?'
quotes: An unnamed canine attempts to understand where food comes from
the narrator researches the question 'Whence does the earth procure this food?'

clues.clue: 'The Great Wall of China': explains piecemeal construction to protect from northern nomads; contains parable 'An Imperial Message' about a herald trapped in the palace
quotes: the narrator of a story by this author relates how the title structure was built piecemeal to protect from northern nomads
a man who journeys for 'thousands of years' to get to 'you, the humble subject,' with a message

clues.clue: 'The Burrow': a creature obsessed with security and silence describes 'moss covering' separating it from the outside; ends in a specific room named 'castle keep'
quotes: a creature obsessed with security and silence tells of the 'moss covering' that separates him from the outside world

The third part of Vertigo describes a period in the life of this author through the lens of his story 'The Hunter Gracchus.'

clues.clue: 'The Cares of a Family Man': about Odradek, a 'flat star-shaped spool for thread'; Zizek calls it 'jouissance embodied'

This author, who wrote of Odradek in 'The Cares of a Family Man'

clues.clue: 'A Little Fable': a mouse laments 'the whole world is growing smaller every day' before being eaten by a cat
quotes: a very short story by this author in which a mouse laments how 'the whole world is growing smaller every day,' only to be eaten by a cat

works.name: Amerika
works.description: Amerika follows Karl Rossmann's arrival in the title country. The first chapter 'The Stoker' was published separately. Karl sees the Statue of Liberty wielding a sword. The last chapter describes the Nature-Theater of Oklahoma, which promises a job to anyone. Critics noted Kafka's debt to Dickens.

quotes: Karl Rossman develops an intimate connection with the title worker aboard a steamship

clues.clue: Gyorgy Lukacs contrasted Kafka with Thomas Mann; critics noted Kafka's debt to Dickens

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Kafka is identified as a Czech/German-language author. Max Brod preserved and published his work posthumously. Kafka wrote a vindictive 100-page Letter to His Father about breaking off his engagement to Felice Bauer. His Zurau Aphorisms include 'A cage went in search of a bird.' Borges' essay 'Kafka and his Precursors' argues writers create their own precursors. W.G. Sebald calls him 'Dr. K' in Vertigo. Georges Bataille discussed his 'perfect puerility.' Camus wrote that his 'whole art consists in forcing the reader to reread.'

clues.clue: Czech / German-language author; works preserved by Max Brod against his wishes
quotes: Max Brod published the works of this author
This author urged his editor Max Brod to destroy the manuscripts for his novels The Trial and The Castle.

clues.clue: Wrote a vindictive 100-page Letter to His Father about breaking off engagement to Felice Bauer
quotes: This author wrote a vindictive 100 page letter to his father that explains why he broke off his engagement to Felice Bauer.

clues.clue: Zurau Aphorisms include 'A cage went in search of a bird'
quotes: The most popular of Kafka's 109 Zurau Aphorisms imagines a cage performing this specific action.

clues.clue: Borges' essay 'Kafka and his Precursors' argues writers create their own precursors; lists Han Yu, Lord Dunsany, and Zeno's paradox
quotes: This author argued that writers create their own precursors in an essay which claims that Browning's 'Fears and Scruples' prophesies Kafka.
lists: a ninth-century apologue by Han Yu, Lord Dunsany's story 'Carcassonne,' and Zeno's paradox as a few of this author's 'precursors'

clues.clue: David Foster Wallace essay 'Some Remarks on Kafka's Funniness' discusses his humor as 'pounding at a door that opens outward'
quotes: This author's humor is described as pounding at a door that 'opens outward: we've been inside what we wanted all along'

clues.clue: Diaries begun at Max Brod's suggestion; contain drafts of first collection Contemplation; discuss inability to marry Felice Bauer
quotes: these works which contain drafts of their author's first published collection, Contemplation. Many of their entries discuss the author's inability to marry Felice Bauer.

clues.clue: Recounted being locked out on a balcony as punishment for crying for water at night
quotes: A letter by this author recounts being locked out on a balcony as a punishment for crying out for water in the middle of the night.

clues.clue: Herta Muller compared to Kafka for similar use of German as a minority language
quotes: the Academy compared her to this other author for their similar use of German as a minority language

summary: Franz Kafka is a German-speaking Czech author known for his novels The Trial, The Castle, and Amerika, and his short fiction including The Metamorphosis, 'In the Penal Colony,' 'A Hunger Artist,' 'A Report to an Academy,' 'Josephine the Singer,' 'The Judgment,' 'Investigations of a Dog,' 'The Burrow,' and many others.
